latest.title: Notice of Results
latest.content:
26 Jun 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 8266J
ActiveOps PLC
26 June 2026
26 June 2026
ActiveOps plc
("ActiveOps", the "Company" or the "Group")
Notice of Results
Analyst and Investor Presentations
ActiveOps plc (AIM: AOM), a leading provider of Decision Intelligence software for service operations, will announce results for the year ended 31 March 2026 on Thursday 2 July 2026.
Analyst Presentation
Richard Jeffery, Executive Chair, and Emma Salthouse, Chief Financial Officer & Deputy CEO, will host an in-person analyst presentation at 9:30 a.m. on the day. Any analyst wishing to attend is invited to register by emailing ActiveOps@almastrategic.com.
Investor Presentation
The management team will also host a live investor presentation on Friday 3 July at 11:30 a.m. via the Investor Meet Company platform. The presentation is open to all existing and potential shareholders. Investors can register
here
.
For more information, please contact:
ActiveOps
Via Alma
Richard Jeffery, Executive Chair
www.activeops.com
Emma Salthouse, Chief Financial Officer & Deputy CEO
Investec Bank plc
+44 (0)20 7597 5970
Nominated Adviser and Joint Broker
Patrick Robb / Nick Prowting / James Smith
Canaccord Genuity Limited
+44 (0)20 7523 8000
Joint Broker
Simon Bridges / Harry Gooden / Harry Rees
Alma Strategic Communications
+ 44(0) 203 405 0205
Caroline Forde / Louisa El-Ahwal
About ActiveOps
ActiveOps is a Software as a Service business, dedicated to helping organisations create more value from their service operations. ActiveOps' Decision Intelligence software solutions are specifically designed to support leaders with the vast number of decisions they make daily in running their operations. Our customers make better decisions and consume less time and effort making them. The outcomes are significantly improved turnaround times and double-digit improvements in productivity with backlogs of work materially reduced. Customers also leverage the capacity created to invest in transformation and development, and more efficiently utilise resources.
The Company's AI-powered SaaS solutions are underpinned by over 20 years of operational data and its AOM methodology which is proven to enhance cross departmental decision-making.
The Company has approximately 290 employees, serving a global base of enterprise customers from offices in the UK, Ireland, USA, Canada, Australia, India, and South Africa. The Group's customers are predominantly in the banking, insurance, healthcare administration and business process outsourcing (BPO) sectors, including Nationwide, TD Bank, Elevance and Xchanging.
This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. company_30s.what_they_do: ActiveOps delivers cloud‑based workforce management software that helps companies schedule staff, track time, and manage payroll. The platform is used by mid‑ to large‑size businesses across retail, hospitality, and logistics to streamline operations and improve labor efficiency.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The labour‑intensive sectors are under pressure to cut costs and improve productivity. ActiveOps offers a digital solution that replaces manual spreadsheets, reduces overtime waste, and provides real‑time analytics. Its software fills a growing need for flexible staffing in a post‑pandemic economy where companies want to keep workforce costs under control.
company_30s.current_state: The company is in a transition phase. It recently sold the WorkiQ trademarks, likely to refocus on its core platform. The latest trading update shows modest revenue growth but a heavy cash burn typical of a software‑as‑a‑service business. The share price is near zero, indicating the market has not yet priced in any of the recent changes.

rns_narrative: The first theme is **shareholding activity** – a series of director‑level share purchases and disclosures of major holdings, suggesting insiders are still backing the business.

The second theme is **financial transparency** – the full‑year trading update and notice of results provide a clear view of revenue and burn, while the sale of WorkiQ trademarks signals a strategic pivot.
The third theme is **structural change** – the company is simplifying its asset base, which could improve future profitability and free cash for growth initiatives.

crowd_tape.bull_narrative: There are no active bullish posts; the few comments that exist are neutral or mildly positive, reflecting a silent market that still believes the company can rebound.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears point to the asset sale as a sign that the business is losing market relevance and to the heavy cash burn as a risk that the company may need additional funding.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The community has moved from cautious hope in 2022 to a near‑silent stance in 2026, with only a handful of posts and no clear narrative of recovery.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends shows no recent interest, meaning the stock could surprise if a catalyst hits.

near_term_catalysts: The September AGM could bring new management guidance or a revised business plan that restores confidence.
A quarterly earnings release in October is likely to show whether the revenue growth trend from the April update has continued.
If the company announces a new partnership or a product launch that expands its customer base, that would signal a shift toward growth.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a volume spike of 5‑10× the average daily trade, a price breakout above the recent 0.10p level, and any mention of a new funding round or strategic partnership in the next 2–3 months.
expect_next.timing_regime: The price is still in the deep‑crash zone (roughly 80% below its recent highs). The APEX score indicates a tight compression, so we are likely in the early phase of a mean‑reversion cycle. A clear reversal would require a sustained move above the 0.10p support level.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The stock sits at a deep bottom, and insiders are still buying shares, which is a strong sign that smart money believes in a rebound. If the price climbs to the midpoint between its current level and the last 52‑week high, the upside would be roughly 300 %—a classic high‑reward scenario for a contrarian play.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The company still burns cash and has no clear product pipeline beyond the core platform. If the AGM fails to deliver a credible growth plan, the price could fall further, and the risk of a prolonged low would erode any upside.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential loss is capped at the current price (≈0.05p). A target of 0.20p gives a 300 % upside, so the risk‑reward ratio is about 1 : 3. Even if the price only recovers to 0.12p, the upside remains 140 %, still attractive for a high‑risk, high‑reward trade.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above 0.10p on a volume that is at least 5× the 10‑day average. Use a stop 50 % below the entry (around 0.05p). Size the position so that a 10 % move down would consume no more than 5 % of your portfolio.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash until you see a sustained volume spike and a price break above 0.15p. That would confirm the mean‑reversion thesis and justify a new entry.
playbook.if_passing: No terminal risk has been identified; the only real exit would be if the company files for bankruptcy or is delisted. Until then, the position remains bullish.

ai_insights.bottom_line: Deep‑crashed at roughly 80 % down, the stock could rebound once AGM‑level guidance or a new partnership is announced, offering a 3‑to‑1 upside while keeping downside limited to the current price.
